sentences.base_language: I love painting in my free time.
sentences.target_language: 暇な時間に絵を描くのが好きです。
sentences.romanized: Hima na jikan ni e wo kaku no ga suki desu.
sentences.literal: Free-time at picture draw nominalizer like.
sentences.grammarNote: の (no) here is a nominalizer — it turns a verb phrase into a noun: 絵を描く (to paint) → 絵を描くの (the act of painting). The pattern 〜のが好き (no ga suki) = 'like doing…' is essential for talking about hobbies.

sentences.base_language: My friend Li Na likes playing the piano.
sentences.target_language: 私の友達の田中さんはピアノを弾くのが好きです。
sentences.romanized: Watashi no tomodachi no Tanaka-san wa piano wo hiku no ga suki desu.
sentences.literal: My friend Tanaka piano play likes.
sentences.grammarNote: 好き (suki) = 'like'. In Japanese, 好き is a な-adjective, not a verb: ピアノが好き (like piano). For activities: 〜のが好き (like doing…). 弾く (hiku) = 'to play (a stringed instrument or keyboard)'. Different from 吹く (fuku, blow/wind instruments).

sentences.base_language: We often go hiking on Saturdays.
sentences.target_language: 私たちはよく土曜日にハイキングに行きます。
sentences.romanized: Watashitachi wa yoku doyōbi ni haikingu ni ikimasu.
sentences.literal: We often Saturday on hiking to go.
sentences.grammarNote: Frequency adverbs like よく (often), 時々 (sometimes), めったに〜ない (rarely), 全然〜ない (never) come before the verb in Japanese. よく (yoku) = 'often' (also 'well' before verbs of ability). Position: always before the main verb.

sentences.target_language: 最近写真撮影を勉強しています。
sentences.romanized: Saikin shashin satsuei wo benkyō shite imasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ています (te imasu) can describe an ongoing activity over a period of time, not just the current moment: 最近勉強しています = 'I've been studying recently / I'm studying these days'. This mirrors the Chinese 最近在学 pattern.

sentences.base_language: How long have you been playing chess?
sentences.target_language: チェスをどのくらいやっていますか？
sentences.romanized: Chesu wo dono kurai yatte imasu ka?
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ています (te imasu) for duration: 三年間やっています = 'have been doing it for three years'. どのくらい (dono kurai) = 'how long / how much'. To state a duration of ongoing experience: 〜ている + time duration + になる/なります.

sentences.base_language: I just started. I'm still a beginner.
sentences.target_language: 始めたばかりです。まだ初心者です。
sentences.romanized: Hajimeta bakari desu. Mada shoshinsha desu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜たばかり (ta bakari) = 'just did (something very recently)'. It attaches to the past tense form: 始めたばかり (just started), 来たばかり (just arrived). This is the Japanese equivalent of 刚 (gāng) in Chinese — recent completion.

sentences.target_language: とても才能がありますね！
sentences.grammarNote: 〜がある (ga aru) expresses having a quality or possession: 才能がある (have talent), センスがある (have good taste), 余裕がある (have room/margin). This is the Japanese equivalent of Chinese 有 + noun for qualities: 有天赋 (yǒu tiānfù).

sentences.base_language: I just enjoy it — it's relaxing.
sentences.target_language: ただ楽しんでいます。リラックスできます。
